chunk_summary: Vitamin A supplementation in SAM is guided by eye findings, measles risk, and prior dosing history, with specific dosages based on age and clinical indications.
learning_objectives: Understand the indications for vitamin A supplementation in severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Learn age-specific dosing guidelines for vitamin A in SAM management.
Recognize emergency management of corneal involvement in vitamin A deficiency.
Differentiate vitamin A therapy from measles vaccination in SAM cases.
key_facts: Vitamin A dosages: 50,000 IU (<6 months), 100,000 IU (6–12 months), 200,000 IU (>12 months).
High-dose vitamin A is given on day 1 for eye signs of deficiency or suspected measles.
Avoid repeat dosing if a high dose was administered in the preceding month unless clinically indicated.
Corneal involvement requires urgent ophthalmic care and vitamin A therapy.
Document vitamin A therapy at discharge to prevent duplicate dosing in outpatient care.
reference_quote: Treat any corneal involvement as an emergency requiring urgent ophthalmic care and vitamin A therapy. - WHO, Pocket book of hospital care for children.
document_key_topics: Measles risk and vitamin A therapy
